Izabel, I want to put a proposal on the table. I suggest acquiring Nordic Sense, the small sensor startup in Campinas.
Interesting. What is the rationale? Before we decide to move, I need to understand the upside and the downside.
The upside is clear: their proprietary tech would let us expand faster. The board is keen on green-lighting a merger this year.
I agree it is worth exploring. But I refuse to rush. We cannot afford to skip due diligence, and I want a proper valuation before committing.
Fair. Do you have a ballpark in mind for what we would pay?
Not yet. I would avoid naming a number before the audit. What matters now is getting buy-in from the other directors.
So what do you suggest doing first?
I propose preparing a short pitch, then meeting the founders. If the numbers hold, I am happy to recommend green-lighting it. Let us aim to decide by the end of the quarter.
The board suggested opening a subsidiary first.
It is worth doing proper due diligence.
We look forward to meeting the founders next week.

Hi Izabel, it is Julian from corporate development, following up on the proposal. Let me lay out the rationale before we decide to move. The idea is simple: I recommend acquiring Nordic Sense, the small sensor startup in Campinas. Their proprietary technology would let us expand into the medical market a full year faster than building it ourselves. That is the upside, and it is significant. Of course, I would avoid rushing. It is worth doing proper due diligence and getting an independent valuation before we commit to anything. The downside, honestly, is integration: merging two engineering cultures always risks slowing both teams down for a quarter or two. My suggestion is that we plan to meet the founders next week, and aim to prepare a short pitch for the board. I really look forward to hearing your read. If you agree it is worth exploring, I will start arranging the first call. Talk soon.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Diana, your CFO, with a quick note on the numbers behind the proposal. I know the board is keen on green-lighting the merger this year, but I would resist committing to a figure too early. First, we cannot afford to skip a proper valuation. Their revenue looks strong, but I want to finish reviewing the last three years before we agree to any ballpark. Second, it is worth remembering that the real cost is not just the price. We would be taking on their debt and their team, so I recommend building a contingency into the model. My advice is to keep the buy-in of the other directors, but to avoid promising a number in the room. Let us aim to present a clean rationale, with upside and downside side by side, and let the board decide. I look forward to walking you through the model on Thursday. Call me if you want to talk it through.
